**Canadians** are people identified with the country of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). This connection may be residential, legal, historical or cultural. For most Canadians, many (or all) of these connections exist and are collectively the source of their being *Canadian*.

Canada is a multilingual and [multicultural](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") society home to people of groups of many different ethnic, religious, and national origins, with the majority of the population made up of [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada") and their descendants. Following the initial period of [French](/wiki/New_France "New France") and then the much larger [British colonization](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas"), different waves (or peaks) of immigration and settlement of non-indigenous peoples took place over the course of nearly two centuries and continue today. Elements of Indigenous, French, British, and more recent immigrant customs, languages, and religions have combined to form the [culture of Canada](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada"), and thus a [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity") and [Canadian values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values"). Canada has also been strongly influenced by its linguistic, geographic, and economic neighbour—the United States.

Canadian independence from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") grew gradually over the course of many years following the formation of the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867. The [First](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") and [Second World Wars](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), in particular, gave rise to a desire among Canadians to have their country recognized as a fully-fledged, sovereign state, with a distinct citizenship. Legislative independence was established with the passage of the [Statute of Westminster, 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster,_1931 "Statute of Westminster, 1931"), the [Canadian Citizenship Act, 1946](/wiki/Canadian_Citizenship_Act,_1946 "Canadian Citizenship Act, 1946"), took effect on January 1, 1947, and full sovereignty was achieved with the [patriation](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation") of the constitution in 1982. Canada's [nationality law](/wiki/Canadian_nationality_law "Canadian nationality law") closely mirrored that of the United Kingdom. Legislation since the mid-20th century represents Canadians' commitment to [multilateralism](/wiki/Multilateralism "Multilateralism") and [socioeconomic development](/wiki/Socioeconomic_development "Socioeconomic development"). Nearly nine in ten (87%) Canadians were proud to identify as Canadian, with over half (61%) expressing they were very proud.[[12]](#cite_note-d680-13)

## Term

The word *Canadian* originally applied, in its French form, *Canadien*, to the colonists residing in the northern part of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France")[[13]](#cite_note-14)— in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")—during the 16th, 17th, and 18th centuries. The French colonists in Maritime Canada (New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and Prince Edward Island), were known as [Acadians](/wiki/Acadians "Acadians").

When [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn") (a son of King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III")) addressed, in English and French, a group of rioters at a poll in [Charlesbourg](/wiki/Charlesbourg,_Quebec_City "Charlesbourg, Quebec City"), [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada") (today Quebec), during the election of the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Lower_Canada "Legislative Assembly of Lower Canada") in June 1792,[[14]](#cite_note-Toffoli33-15) he stated, "I urge you to unanimity and concord. Let me hear no more of the odious distinction of English and French. You are all His Britannic Majesty's beloved Canadian subjects."[[15]](#cite_note-16) It was the first-known use of the term *Canadian* to mean both [French](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") and [English](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") settlers in [the Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas").[[14]](#cite_note-Toffoli33-15)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Population

See also: [Population of Canada](/wiki/Population_of_Canada "Population of Canada") and [Demographics of Canada](/wiki/Demographics_of_Canada "Demographics of Canada")

As of 2010, Canadians make up 0.5% of the [world's total population](/wiki/World_population "World population"),[[17]](#cite_note-18) having relied upon immigration for population growth and social development.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorneliusTsudaMartinHollifield2004100-19) Approximately 41% of current Canadians are first- or second-generation immigrants,[[19]](#cite_note-immigrantclass-20) and 20% of Canadian residents in the 2000s were not born in the country.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBybeeMcCrae200992-21) [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada") projects that, by 2031, nearly one-half of Canadians above the age of 15 will be [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born") or have one foreign-born parent.[[21]](#cite_note-22) [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada"), according to the [2016 Canadian census](/wiki/Canada_2016_Census "Canada 2016 Census"), numbered at 1,673,780 or 4.9% of the country's 35,151,728 population.[[22]](#cite_note-firstnations-23)

### Immigration

Main articles: [Immigration to Canada](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada"), [Canada immigration statistics](/wiki/Canada_immigration_statistics "Canada immigration statistics"), and [Annual immigration statistics of Canada](/wiki/Annual_immigration_statistics_of_Canada "Annual immigration statistics of Canada")

While the first contact with Europeans and [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") peoples in Canada had occurred a century or more before, the first group of permanent settlers were the [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"), who founded the [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") settlements, in present-day [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"); and [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), in present-day [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), during the early part of the 17th century.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHudson200215-24)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffiths20054-25)

Approximately 100 [Irish-born families](/wiki/Irish_Quebecers "Irish Quebecers") would settle the [Saint Lawrence Valley](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_River "Saint Lawrence River") by 1700, assimilating into the *Canadien* population and culture.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGowan1999-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1999736ff-27) During the 18th and 19th century; immigration westward (to the area known as [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land")) was carried out by "[Voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs")"; French settlers working for the [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company"); and by [British](/wiki/British_people "British people") settlers ([English](/wiki/English_people "English people") and [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people")) representing the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"), coupled with independent entrepreneurial woodsman called [coureur des bois](/wiki/Coureur_des_bois "Coureur des bois").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStandford200042-28) This arrival of newcomers led to the creation of the [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_people_(Canada) "Métis people (Canada)"), an ethnic group of mixed European and [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") parentage.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorrows2010134-29)

In the wake of the British [Conquest of New France](/wiki/Conquest_of_1760 "Conquest of 1760") in 1760 and the [Expulsion of the Acadians](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians"), many families from the British colonies in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") moved over into Nova Scotia and other colonies in Canada, where the British made farmland available to British settlers on easy terms. More settlers arrived during and after the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), when approximately 60,000 [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") fled to [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America"), a large portion of whom settled in New Brunswick.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrinJohnsonMcPhersonFahs2007172-30) After the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), British (including British army regulars), Scottish, and Irish immigration was encouraged throughout Rupert's Land, [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFeltes199919-31)

Between 1815 and 1850, some 800,000 immigrants came to the colonies of British North America, mainly from the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") as part of the [Great Migration of Canada](/wiki/Great_Migration_of_Canada "Great Migration of Canada").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarland-Jacobs2007177-32) These new arrivals included some [Gaelic-speaking](/wiki/Canadian_Gaelic "Canadian Gaelic") [Highland Scots](/wiki/Highland_Scots "Highland Scots") displaced by the [Highland Clearances](/wiki/Highland_Clearances "Highland Clearances") to Nova Scotia.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampey2008122-33) The [Great Famine of Ireland](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") of the 1840s significantly increased the pace of Irish immigration to [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island") and the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), with over 35,000 distressed individuals landing in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") in 1847 and 1848.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGowan200997-34)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElliott2004106-35) Descendants of Francophone and Anglophone northern Europeans who arrived in the 17th, 18th, and 19th centuries are often referred to as [Old Stock Canadians](/wiki/Old_Stock_Canadians "Old Stock Canadians").[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Beginning in the late 1850s, the immigration of Chinese into the [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island") and [Colony of British Columbia](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1858%E2%80%931866) "Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)") peaked with the onset of the [Fraser Canyon Gold Rush](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon_Gold_Rush "Fraser Canyon Gold Rush").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHallHwang20019-38) The *[Chinese Immigration Act of 1885](/wiki/Chinese_Immigration_Act_of_1885 "Chinese Immigration Act of 1885")* eventually placed a head tax on all Chinese immigrants, in hopes of discouraging Chinese immigration after completion of the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuang2006107-39) Additionally, growing [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asians "South Asians") immigration into British Columbia during the early 1900s[[39]](#cite_note-SinghHirap94-40) led to the [continuous journey regulation](/wiki/Continuous_journey_regulation "Continuous journey regulation") act of 1908 which indirectly halted [Indian](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people") immigration to Canada, as later evidenced by the infamous 1914 [*Komagata Maru* incident](/wiki/Komagata_Maru_incident "Komagata Maru incident").

The [population of Canada](/wiki/Population_of_Canada "Population of Canada") has consistently risen, doubling approximately every 40 years, since the establishment of the [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867.[[41]](#cite_note-43) In the mid-to-late 19th century, Canada had a policy of assisting [immigrants from Europe](/wiki/European_Canadian "European Canadian"), including an estimated 100,000 unwanted "[Home Children](/wiki/Home_Children "Home Children")" from Britain.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHobbsMacKechnieLavalette199933-44) [Block settlement](/wiki/Block_settlement "Block settlement") communities were established throughout [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada") between the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Some were planned and others were spontaneously created by the settlers themselves.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartens200428-45) Canada received mainly European immigrants, predominantly [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians"), Germans, [Scandinavians](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_people "Dutch people"), [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDay2000124-46)
Legislative restrictions on immigration (such as the continuous journey regulation and *[Chinese Immigration Act, 1923](/wiki/Chinese_Immigration_Act,_1923 "Chinese Immigration Act, 1923")*) that had favoured British and other European immigrants were amended in the 1960s, opening the doors to immigrants from all parts of the world.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKsenychLiu2001407-47) While the 1950s had still seen high levels of immigration by Europeans, by the 1970s immigrants were increasingly Chinese, Indian, [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people"), [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaicans "Jamaicans"), and [Haitian](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti").[[46]](#cite_note-Multicultural-48) During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Canada received many [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans") [Vietnam War draft dissenters](/wiki/Draft_evasion_in_the_Vietnam_War "Draft evasion in the Vietnam War").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKusch20012-49) Throughout the late 1980s and 1990s, Canada's growing Pacific trade brought with it a large influx of [South Asians](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_South_Asia "Ethnic groups in South Asia"), who tended to settle in [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAgnew2007182-50) Immigrants of all backgrounds tend to settle in the [major urban centres](/wiki/List_of_census_metropolitan_areas_and_agglomerations_in_Canada "List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilkinson1980200-51)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGood200913-52) The Canadian public, as well as the major political parties, are tolerant of immigrants.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHollifieldMartinOrrenius201411-53)

The majority of [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration "Illegal immigration") come from the [southern provinces of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Administrative divisions of the People's Republic of China"), with Asia as a whole, [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneider2009367-54) Estimates of numbers of illegal immigrants range between 35,000 and 120,000.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

### Citizenship and diaspora

[Canadian citizenship](/wiki/Canadian_nationality_law "Canadian nationality law") is typically obtained by birth in Canada or by birth or adoption abroad when at least one biological parent or adoptive parent is a Canadian citizen who was born in Canada or naturalized in Canada (and did not receive citizenship by being born outside of Canada to a Canadian citizen).[[54]](#cite_note-parent-56) It can also be granted to a [permanent resident](/wiki/Permanent_resident_(Canada) "Permanent resident (Canada)") who lives in Canada for three out of four years and meets specific requirements.[[55]](#cite_note-rights-57) Canada [established its own nationality law](/wiki/History_of_Canadian_nationality_law "History of Canadian nationality law") in 1946, with the enactment of the *[Canadian Citizenship Act](/wiki/Canadian_Citizenship_Act_1946 "Canadian Citizenship Act 1946")* which took effect on January 1, 1947.[[56]](#cite_note-58) The *[Immigration and Refugee Protection Act](/wiki/Immigration_and_Refugee_Protection_Act "Immigration and Refugee Protection Act")* was passed by the [Parliament of Canada](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada") in 2001 as Bill C-11, which replaced the *[Immigration Act, 1976](/wiki/Immigration_Act,_1976 "Immigration Act, 1976")* as the primary federal legislation regulating immigration.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Prior to the conferring of legal status on Canadian citizenship, Canada's naturalization laws consisted of a multitude of Acts beginning with the *Immigration Act* of 1910.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloemraad2006269-60)

According to [Citizenship and Immigration Canada](/wiki/Citizenship_and_Immigration_Canada "Citizenship and Immigration Canada"), there are three main classifications for immigrants: *family class* (persons closely related to Canadian residents), *economic class* (admitted on the basis of a point system that accounts for age, health and labour-market skills required for cost effectively inducting the immigrants into Canada's labour market) and *refugee class* (those seeking protection by applying to remain in the country by way of the [Canadian immigration and refugee law](/wiki/Canadian_immigration_and_refugee_law "Canadian immigration and refugee law")).[[59]](#cite_note-61) In 2008, there were 65,567 immigrants in the family class, 21,860 refugees, and 149,072 economic immigrants amongst the 247,243 total immigrants to the country.[[19]](#cite_note-immigrantclass-20) Canada resettles over one in 10 of the world's [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee")[[60]](#cite_note-62) and has one of the highest per-capita immigration rates in the world.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman200851-63)

As of a 2010 report by the [Asia Pacific Foundation of Canada](/wiki/Asia_Pacific_Foundation_of_Canada "Asia Pacific Foundation of Canada"), there were 2.8 million [Canadian citizens abroad](/wiki/Canadian_diaspora "Canadian diaspora").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeVoretz2011-64) This represents about 8% of the total Canadian population. Of those living abroad, the United States, Hong Kong, the United Kingdom, Taiwan, China, Lebanon, United Arab Emirates, and Australia have the largest Canadian diaspora. Canadians in the United States constitute the greatest single expatriate community at over 1 million in 2009, representing 35.8% of all Canadians abroad.[[63]](#cite_note-65) Under current [Canadian law](/wiki/Canadian_law "Canadian law"), Canada does not restrict [dual citizenship](/wiki/Dual_citizenship "Dual citizenship"), but [Passport Canada](/wiki/Passport_Canada "Passport Canada") encourages its citizens to travel abroad on their [Canadian passport](/wiki/Canadian_passport "Canadian passport") so that they can access Canadian [consular services](/wiki/Consulate "Consulate").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray2010302-66)

Main article: [Ethnic origins of people in Canada](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada")

According to the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), over 450 "[ethnic or cultural origins](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada")" were self-reported by Canadians.[[4]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-4) The major [panethnic](/wiki/Panethnicity "Panethnicity") origin groups in Canada are: [European](/wiki/European_Canadians "European Canadians") (52.5%), [North American](/wiki/North_America "North America") (22.9%), [Asian](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Asia "Ethnic groups in Asia") (19.3%), [North American Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") (6.1%), [African](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (3.8%), [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America") (2.5%), [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_people "Caribbean people") (2.1%), [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceanian_people "Oceanian people") (0.3%), and Other (6%).[[4]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-4)[[65]](#cite_note-ethnicpopulation2021-68) Statistics Canada reports that 35.5% of the population reported multiple ethnic origins, thus the overall total is greater than 100%.[[4]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-4)[[d]](#cite_note-ethnic-69)

The country's ten largest self-reported specific ethnic or cultural origins in 2021 were [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_ethnicity "Canadian ethnicity")[[c]](#cite_note-Canadian-67) (accounting for 15.6 percent of the population), followed by [English](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") (14.7 percent), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Canadians "Irish Canadians") (12.1 percent), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Canadians "Scottish Canadians") (12.1 percent), [French](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") (11.0 percent), [German](/wiki/German_Canadians "German Canadians") (8.1 percent),[Indian](/wiki/Indo-Canadians "Indo-Canadians") (5.1 percent),[[e]](#cite_note-Indo-Canadian-74) [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians") (4.7 percent), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Canadians "Italian Canadians") (4.3 percent), and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_Canadians "Ukrainian Canadians") (3.5 percent).[[69]](#cite_note-ethnicpopulationordered2021-73)[[65]](#cite_note-ethnicpopulation2021-68)

Of the 36.3 million people enumerated in 2021 approximately 25.4 million reported being "[white](/wiki/White_people "White people")", representing 69.8 percent of the population.[[2]](#cite_note-2021newsreleaseB-2)[[70]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022-75)[[71]](#cite_note-populationgroups2021-76) The indigenous population representing 5 percent or 1.8 million individuals, grew by 9.4 percent compared to the non-Indigenous population, which grew by 5.3 percent from 2016 to 2021.[[72]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022a-77) One out of every four Canadians or 26.5 percent of the population belonged to a non-White and non-Indigenous [visible minority](/wiki/Visible_minority "Visible minority"),[[71]](#cite_note-populationgroups2021-76)[[f]](#cite_note-79) the largest of which in 2021 were [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians") (2.6 million people; 7.1 percent), Chinese (1.7 million; 4.7 percent) and [Black](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (1.5 million; 4.3 percent).[[70]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022-75)

Between 2011 and 2016, the visible minority population rose by 18.4 percent.[[74]](#cite_note-Census_Profile,_2016_Census-80) In 1961, less than two percent of Canada's population (about 300,000 people) were members of visible minority groups.[[75]](#cite_note-81) The 2021 Census indicated that
8.3 million people, or almost one-quarter (23.0 percent) of the population reported themselves as being or having been a [landed immigrant or permanent resident](/wiki/Permanent_residency_in_Canada "Permanent residency in Canada") in Canada—above the [1921 Census](/wiki/1921_Canadian_Census "1921 Canadian Census") previous record of 22.3 percent.[[76]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022b-82) In 2021 India, China, and the Philippines were the top three countries of origin for immigrants moving to Canada.[[77]](#cite_note-83)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Canada](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Canflagcartoon.jpg/250px-Canflagcartoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canflagcartoon.jpg)

A 1911 political cartoon on Canada's bicultural identity showing a flag combining symbols of Britain, France and Canada; titled "The next favor. 'A flag to suit the minority.'"

[Canadian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada") is primarily a [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), with influences by First Nations and other cultures. It is a product of its [ethnicities](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada"), [languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada "Languages of Canada"), [religions](/wiki/Religion_in_Canada "Religion in Canada"), [political](/wiki/Politics_of_Canada "Politics of Canada"), and [legal system](/wiki/Law_of_Canada "Law of Canada")(s). Canada has been shaped by waves of migration that have combined to form a unique blend of [art](/wiki/Canadian_art "Canadian art"), [cuisine](/wiki/Canadian_cuisine "Canadian cuisine"), [literature](/wiki/Canadian_literature "Canadian literature"), [humour](/wiki/Canadian_humour "Canadian humour"), and [music](/wiki/Music_of_Canada "Music of Canada").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKalman20094–7-84) Today, Canada has a diverse makeup of nationalities and constitutional protection for policies that promote [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism "Multiculturalism") rather than [cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeRoccoChabot200813-85) In Quebec, cultural identity is strong, and many French-speaking commentators speak of a [Quebec culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec") distinct from English Canadian culture.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFranklinBaun199561-86) However, as a whole, Canada is a [cultural mosaic](/wiki/Cultural_mosaic "Cultural mosaic"): a collection of several regional, indigenous, and ethnic subcultures.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEnglish2004111-87)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurgess200531-88)

Canadian government policies such as [official bilingualism](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada"); [publicly funded health care](/wiki/Health_care_in_Canada "Health care in Canada"); [higher and more progressive taxation](/wiki/Income_taxes_in_Canada "Income taxes in Canada"); outlawing [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Canada "Capital punishment in Canada"); strong efforts to eliminate [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Canada "Poverty in Canada"); strict [gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_Canada "Gun politics in Canada"); the legalizing of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Canada "Same-sex marriage in Canada"), [pregnancy terminations](/wiki/Abortion_in_Canada "Abortion in Canada"), [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia_in_Canada "Euthanasia in Canada") and [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_in_Canada "Cannabis in Canada") are social indicators of Canada's political and [cultural values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrickerWright200516-89)[[84]](#cite_note-90) [American media](/wiki/American_media "American media") and entertainment are popular, if not dominant, in English Canada; conversely, many Canadian cultural products and entertainers are successful in the United States and worldwide.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlackwell2005-91) The [Government of Canada](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") has also influenced culture with programs, laws, and institutions. It has created [Crown corporations](/wiki/Crown_corporations_of_Canada "Crown corporations of Canada") to promote Canadian culture through media, and has also tried to [protect Canadian culture](/wiki/Canadian_cultural_protectionism "Canadian cultural protectionism") by setting legal minimums on [Canadian content](/wiki/Canadian_content "Canadian content").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmstrong2010144-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Outside_Union_Station.jpg/250px-Outside_Union_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Outside_Union_Station.jpg)

*Monument to Multiculturalism* by Francesco Pirelli in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"); four identical sculptures are located in [Buffalo City](/wiki/Buffalo_City_Local_Municipality "Buffalo City Local Municipality"), [Changchun](/wiki/Changchun "Changchun"), [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")

Canadian culture has historically been influenced by [European culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Europe "Culture of Europe") and traditions, especially [British](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom") and [French](/wiki/Culture_of_France "Culture of France"), and by its own [indigenous cultures](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada#Culture "Indigenous peoples in Canada"). Most of Canada's territory was inhabited and developed later than other European colonies in the Americas, with the result that themes and symbols of pioneers, trappers, and traders were important in the early development of the [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity").[[87]](#cite_note-pioneer_lit-93) First Nations played a critical part in the development of [European colonies in Canada](/wiki/Former_colonies_and_territories_in_Canada "Former colonies and territories in Canada"), particularly for their role in assisting exploration of the continent during the [North American fur trade](/wiki/North_American_fur_trade "North American fur trade").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhiteFindlay199967-94) The British conquest of New France in the mid-1700s brought a large [Francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone") population under [British Imperial rule](/wiki/Canada_under_British_Imperial_control "Canada under British Imperial control"), creating a need for compromise and accommodation.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDufour199025-95) The new British rulers left alone much of the religious, political, and social culture of the French-speaking *[habitants](/wiki/Habitants "Habitants")*, guaranteeing through the *[Quebec Act](/wiki/Quebec_Act "Quebec Act")* of 1774 the right of the *Canadiens* to practise the Catholic faith and to use [French civil law](/wiki/Law_of_France "Law of France") (now [Quebec law](/wiki/Quebec_law "Quebec law")).[[90]](#cite_note-96)

The *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")* was designed to meet the growing calls of Canadians for autonomy from British rule, while avoiding the overly strong decentralization that contributed to the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") in the United States.[[91]](#cite_note-civil_war-97) The compromises made by the [Fathers of Confederation](/wiki/Fathers_of_Confederation "Fathers of Confederation") set Canadians on a path to [bilingualism](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism"), and this in turn contributed to an acceptance of diversity.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVaillancourtCoche200911-98)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi20023-99)

The [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") and overall civilian participation in the [First World War](/wiki/Military_history_of_Canada_during_World_War_I "Military history of Canada during World War I") and [Second World War](/wiki/Military_history_of_Canada_during_World_War_II "Military history of Canada during World War II") helped to foster [Canadian nationalism](/wiki/Canadian_nationalism "Canadian nationalism"),[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENersessian2007-100)[[95]](#cite_note-CIC22-101) however, in 1917 and 1944, [conscription crisis'](/wiki/Conscription_Crisis_of_1917 "Conscription Crisis of 1917") highlighted the considerable rift along ethnic lines between Anglophones and Francophones.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELinteauDurocherRobert1983522-102) As a result of the First and Second World Wars, the Government of Canada became more assertive and less deferential to British authority.[[97]](#cite_note-103) With the gradual loosening of political ties to the United Kingdom and the modernization of Canadian immigration policies, 20th-century immigrants with [African](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_of_Africa "Ethnic groups of Africa"), Caribbean and [Asian](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Asia "Ethnic groups in Asia") nationalities have added to the Canadian identity and its culture.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBodvarssonVan_den_Berg2009380-104) The multiple-origins immigration pattern continues today, with the arrival of large numbers of immigrants from non-British or non-French backgrounds.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrato200950-105)

[Multiculturalism in Canada](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada "Multiculturalism in Canada") was adopted as the official policy of the government during the premiership of [Pierre Trudeau](/wiki/Pierre_Trudeau "Pierre Trudeau") in the 1970s and 1980s.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuncanLey1993205-106) The Canadian government has often been described as the instigator of multicultural ideology, because of its public emphasis on the [social importance of immigration](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_immigration_to_Canada "Economic impact of immigration to Canada").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayland1997-107) Multiculturalism is administered by the [Department of Citizenship and Immigration](/wiki/Department_of_Citizenship_and_Immigration_Canada "Department of Citizenship and Immigration Canada") and reflected in the law through the *[Canadian Multiculturalism Act](/wiki/Canadian_Multiculturalism_Act "Canadian Multiculturalism Act")*[[102]](#cite_note-108) and [section 27 of the *Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms*](/wiki/Section_27_of_the_Canadian_Charter_of_Rights_and_Freedoms "Section 27 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms").[[103]](#cite_note-109)

### Values

Main article: [Canadian values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values")

[Canadian values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values") are the perceived commonly shared ethical and human values of Canadians.[[104]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_Douglas_Baer_1993-110) The majority of Canadians believe they share specific values,[[105]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_d680-111)[[106]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_j676-112) with a plurality identifying [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Canada "Human rights in Canada"), [respect for the law](/wiki/Law_of_Canada "Law of Canada") and [gender equality](/wiki/Feminism_in_Canada "Feminism in Canada") as collective principles.[[107]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_u440-113)[[108]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_v187-114) Canadians generally exhibit pride in [equality before the law](/wiki/Equality_before_the_law "Equality before the law"), fairness, [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), freedom, and respect for others;[[109]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_h799-115) while often making personal decisions based on self-interests rather than a collective [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity").[[110]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_g083-116) Tolerance and sensitivity hold significant importance in [Canada's multicultural society](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada "Multiculturalism in Canada"), as does politeness.[[110]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_g083-116)[[108]](#cite_note-Canadian_values_v187-114)

### Identity

Main article: [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity")

[Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity") refers to the unique culture, characteristics and condition of being Canadian, as well as the many symbols and expressions that set [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") and Canadians apart from other peoples and cultures of the world. Changes in demographics, history, and social interactions have led to alterations in the Canadian identity over time. This identity is not fixed; as [Canadian values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values") evolve they impact Canadians' social integration, civic engagement, and connections with one another.[[111]](#cite_note-Canadian_identity_l552-117) In Quebec, identity is strong and there is a [French Canadian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec") that is distinct from [English Canadian](/wiki/English_Canada "English Canada") and Indigenous identities.[[112]](#cite_note-118) Nonetheless, as a whole, [Canadian multiculturalism](/wiki/Canadian_multiculturalism "Canadian multiculturalism"), is in theory a [cultural mosaic](/wiki/Cultural_mosaic "Cultural mosaic") of regional ethnic subcultures and diverse areas including [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada#Settlement "Multiculturalism in Canada"),[[113]](#cite_note-119) with nearly nine in ten (87%) Canadians being proud to identify as Canadian, with over half (61%) expressing they were very proud.[[114]](#cite_note-Canadian_identity_d680-120)

Nearly nine in ten (87%) Canadians were proud to identify as Canadian, with over half (61%) expressing they were very proud. The highest pride levels were for [Canadian history](/wiki/History_of_Canada "History of Canada") (70%), the [armed forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces") (64%), the [health care system](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Canada "Healthcare in Canada") (64%), and the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") (63%). However, pride in Canada’s political influence was lower at 46%. Outside Quebec, pride ranged from 91% in British Columbia to 94% in Prince Edward Island, while 70% of Quebec residents felt proud. Seniors and women showed the most pride, especially among first- and second-generation immigrants, who valued both Canadian identity and achievements.[[12]](#cite_note-d680-13)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Canada](/wiki/Religion_in_Canada "Religion in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Freedom_of_Religion_%2838007857792%29.jpg/250px-Freedom_of_Religion_%2838007857792%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Freedom_of_Religion_(38007857792).jpg)

Freedom of religion sculpture by Marlene Hilton Moore at the McMurtry Gardens of Justice in Toronto[[115]](#cite_note-McMurtry_Gardens_of_Justice-121)

[Religion in Canada](/wiki/Religion_in_Canada "Religion in Canada") encompasses a wide range of beliefs and customs that historically has been dominated by [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[116]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_HalesLauzon2009-122)[[117]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Cornelissen_2021-123) The [constitution of Canada](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") refers to 'God'; however Canada has no official church and the government is officially committed to [religious pluralism](/wiki/Religious_pluralism "Religious pluralism").[[118]](#cite_note-124) [Freedom of religion in Canada](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Canada "Freedom of religion in Canada") is a constitutionally protected right, allowing individuals to assemble and worship without limitation or interference.[[119]](#cite_note-125) Rates of religious adherence have steadily decreased since the 1960s.[[117]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Cornelissen_2021-123) After having once been central and integral to Canadian culture and daily life,[[120]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Roberts2005w-126) Canada has become a [post-Christian](/wiki/Postchristianity "Postchristianity") state.[[121]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_BramadatSeljak2009-127)[[122]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Bowen2004-128)[[123]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_GregoryJohnston2009-129) Although the majority of Canadians consider [religion to be unimportant](/wiki/Importance_of_religion_by_country "Importance of religion by country") in their daily lives,[[124]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Punnett2015-130) they still believe in God.[[125]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_Haskell2009-131) The practice of religion is generally considered a private matter throughout society and the state.[[126]](#cite_note-Religion_in_Canada_BoyleSheen2013-132)

### Symbols

Main article: [National symbols of Canada](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Canada "National symbols of Canada")

Themes of nature, pioneers, trappers, and traders played an important part in the early development of Canadian symbolism.[[127]](#cite_note-133) Modern symbols emphasize the country's geography, northern climate, lifestyles, and the Canadianization of traditional European and Indigenous symbols.[[128]](#cite_note-134) The use of the [maple leaf](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") as a symbol dates to the early 18th century in New France.[[129]](#cite_note-Canada_g606-135) The maple leaf is depicted on Canada's [current](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") and [previous flags](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign") and on the [arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada").[[130]](#cite_note-Canada_symbol1-136) Canada's official tartan, known as the "[maple leaf tartan](/wiki/Regional_tartans_of_Canada "Regional tartans of Canada")", reflects the colours of the maple leaf through the seasons—green in the [spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)"), gold in the early [autumn](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn"), red at the first [frost](/wiki/Frost "Frost"), and brown after falling.[[131]](#cite_note-137) The arms of Canada are closely modelled after [those of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom"), with French and distinctive Canadian elements replacing or added to those derived from the British version.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Canada](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada "Languages of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Bilinguisme_au_Canada-fr.svg/330px-Bilinguisme_au_Canada-fr.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bilinguisme_au_Canada-fr.svg)

Approximately 98% of Canadians can speak English or French (2006)[[133]](#cite_note-Highlights-139) *English – 56.9%

English and French (Bilingual) – 16.1%

French – 21.3%

Sparsely populated area (<0.4 km2 (0.15 sq mi) per person)*

A multitude of languages are used by Canadians, with [English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") and [French](/wiki/Canadian_French "Canadian French") (the [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language")) being the [mother tongues](/wiki/First_language "First language") of approximately 56% and 21% of Canadians, respectively.[[134]](#cite_note-Highlightsb-140) As of the 2016 Census, just over 7.3 million Canadians listed a non-official language as their mother tongue. Some of the most common non-official first languages include Chinese (1,227,680 first-language speakers), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (501,680), Spanish (458,850), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") (431,385), Arabic (419,895), German (384,040), and Italian (375,645).[[134]](#cite_note-Highlightsb-140) Less than one percent of Canadians (just over 250,000 individuals) can speak an [indigenous language](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada#Indigenous_languages "Languages of Canada"). About half this number (129,865) reported using an indigenous language on a daily basis.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGordon2005-141) Additionally, Canadians speak several [sign languages](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language"); the number of speakers is unknown of the most spoken ones, [American Sign Language](/wiki/American_Sign_Language "American Sign Language") (ASL) and [Quebec Sign Language](/wiki/Quebec_Sign_Language "Quebec Sign Language") (LSQ),[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKockaertSteurs2015490-142) as it is of [Maritime Sign Language](/wiki/Maritime_Sign_Language "Maritime Sign Language") and [Plains Sign Talk](/wiki/Plains_Indian_Sign_Language "Plains Indian Sign Language").[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrimesGrimes2000-143) There are only 47 speakers of the Inuit sign language [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuit_Sign_Language "Inuit Sign Language").[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchuitBakerPfau2011-144)

English and French are recognized by the [Constitution of Canada](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") as official languages.[[139]](#cite_note-Languages-145) All federal government laws are thus enacted in both English and French, with government services available in both languages.[[139]](#cite_note-Languages-145) Two of Canada's territories give official status to indigenous languages. In [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"), [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut "Inuktitut"), and [Inuinnaqtun](/wiki/Inuinnaqtun_language "Inuinnaqtun language") are official languages, alongside the national languages of English and French, and Inuktitut is a common vehicular language in territorial government.[[140]](#cite_note-146) In the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), the *[Official Languages Act](/wiki/Official_Languages_Act_(Northwest_Territories) "Official Languages Act (Northwest Territories)")* declares that there are eleven different languages: [Chipewyan](/wiki/Dene_Suline_language "Dene Suline language"), [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language"), English, French, [Gwich'in](/wiki/Gwich%27in_language "Gwich'in language"), Inuinnaqtun, Inuktitut, [Inuvialuktun](/wiki/Inuvialuktun "Inuvialuktun"), [North Slavey](/wiki/Slavey_language "Slavey language"), [South Slavey](/wiki/Slavey_language "Slavey language"), and [Tłįchǫ](/wiki/Dogrib_language "Dogrib language").[[141]](#cite_note-147) [Multicultural media](/wiki/Multicultural_media_in_Canada "Multicultural media in Canada") are widely accessible across the country and offer specialty television channels, newspapers, and other publications in many minority languages.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaGanahl200662-148)

In Canada, as elsewhere in the world of [European colonies](/wiki/History_of_colonialism "History of colonialism"), the frontier of European exploration and settlement tended to be a linguistically diverse and fluid place, as cultures using different languages met and interacted. The need for a common means of communication between the indigenous inhabitants and new arrivals for the purposes of trade, and (in some cases) intermarriage, led to the development of [mixed languages](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language").[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinford2003183-149) Languages like [Michif](/wiki/Michif_language "Michif language"), [Chinook Jargon](/wiki/Chinook_Jargon "Chinook Jargon"), and [Bungi creole](/wiki/Bungi_creole "Bungi creole") tended to be highly localized and were often spoken by only a small number of individuals who were frequently capable of speaking another language.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWurmMuhlhauslerTyron19961491-150) [Plains Sign Talk](/wiki/Plains_Sign_Talk "Plains Sign Talk")—which functioned originally as a trade language used to communicate internationally and across linguistic borders—reached across Canada, the United States, and into Mexico.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPfauSteinbachWoll2012540-151)
